content:
# Acts Chapter 18 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 247-248 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_018_page_001.jpg, ACT_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** After these things, having departed from Athens, he came to Corinth.

**2** And finding a certain Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus by birth, who had recently come from Italy, and Priscilla his wife -- because Claudius had commanded all the Jews to depart from Rome -- he came to them,

**3** and because he was of the same trade, he stayed with them and they worked, for they were tentmakers by trade.

**4** And he was reasoning in the synagogue every Sabbath, and was persuading both Jews and Greeks.

**5** And when both Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul was constrained by the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ.

**6** And when they opposed and blasphemed, he shook out his garments and said to them, "Your blood be upon your own head; I am clean. From now on I will go to the Gentiles."

**7** And having departed from there, he went into the house of a certain man named Titius Justus, a worshiper of God, whose house was adjoining the synagogue.

**8** And Crispus, the ruler of the synagogue, believed in the Lord with his whole household; and many of the Corinthians, hearing, were believing and being baptized.

**9** And the Lord said to Paul through a vision in the night, "Do not be afraid, but speak, and do not be silent,

**10** because I am with you, and no one will attack you to harm you, because there are many people belonging to me in this city."

**11** And he stayed a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.

**12** And when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews with one accord rose up against Paul and brought him before the judgment seat,

**13** saying, "This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law."

**14** And when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, "If indeed it were some wrongdoing or wicked villainy, O Jews, I would with reason bear with you;

**15** but if these are questions about a word and names and the law among you, see to it yourselves. I do not wish to be a judge of these things."

**16** And he drove them from the judgment seat.

**17** And they all, having seized Sosthenes the ruler of the synagogue, were beating him before the judgment seat. And none of these things was of concern to Gallio.

**18** And Paul, having remained yet many days, took leave of the brothers and sailed away to Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila, having shorn his head in Cenchreae, for he had a vow.

**19** And they came to Ephesus, and he left them there. And he himself, having entered into the synagogue, reasoned with the Jews.

**20** And when they asked him to remain for a longer time, he did not consent,

**21** but took leave of them, saying, "I will return to you again, God willing." And he set sail from Ephesus.

**22** And having come down to Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, and went down to Antioch.

**23** And having spent some time, he departed, passing through the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.

**24** Now a certain Jew named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth, an eloquent man, arrived at Ephesus, being powerful in the Scriptures.

**25** This man had been instructed in the way of the Lord, and being fervent in spirit, he was speaking and teaching accurately the things concerning Jesus, though knowing only the baptism of John.

**26** And this man began to speak boldly in the synagogue. And when Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him aside and more accurately expounded to him the way of God.

**27** And when he wished to cross over into Achaia, the brothers encouraged him and wrote to the disciples to welcome him; who, having arrived, greatly helped those who had believed through grace,

**28** for he was powerfully refuting the Jews publicly, showing through the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 18 narrates three interconnected episodes in Paul's second missionary journey: his eighteen-month residence in Corinth and the founding of the church there (vv. 1-17), his departure through Cenchreae and Ephesus to Antioch and back through Galatia (vv. 18-23), and the introduction of Apollos at Ephesus (vv. 24-28). The chapter is anchored historically by the reference to Gallio as proconsul of Achaia (v. 12), datable to ca. 51-52 AD via the Delphi inscription, making it one of the most important chronological fixed points in the New Testament.

**Manuscript condition:** The text of Acts 18 begins on page 247 (image 001), where Acts 18:3 is marked at the bottom right column. The text continues onto page 248 (image 002), with column markers at Acts 18:9, 18:17, and 18:24. The text is well preserved in the primary scribal hand with clear scriptio continua throughout. Standard nomina sacra are visible: IHN/IS (Jesus), XN/XS (Christ), KW/KS (Lord), QU/QS (God). The chapter concludes before Acts 19:2 in the rightmost column of page 248.

**Key textual issue -- verse 5:** The most significant textual variant in this chapter is the Sinaiticus reading SUNEIXETO TW LOGW ("was constrained by the word") in v. 5, where the Byzantine tradition reads TW PNEUMATI ("by the spirit"). All three translators correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading, though with varying degrees of dynamism in rendering. SOPHIA preserves "constrained by the word" as the closest formal rendering of the manuscript witness.

**Key textual issue -- verse 21:** Sinaiticus preserves a shorter text at v. 21, omitting the phrase "I must by all means keep the coming feast in Jerusalem" found in the Textus Receptus. This omission is confirmed by the manuscript and supported by P74, Alexandrinus, and Vaticanus. All three translators correctly follow the shorter reading.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show broad agreement on the narrative framework of this chapter. The primary areas of divergence involve: (1) degrees of dynamism in rendering Paul's emotional state (v. 5), (2) word choice in Gallio's judicial speech (vv. 14-15), (3) the ambiguous subject of the beating in v. 17, (4) the hair-shaving reference in v. 18, (5) characterization of Apollos (vv. 24-25), and (6) the order and rendering of Priscilla/Aquila in v. 26.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "After these-[things]" vs. "After these things" vs. "After this" | "After these-[things]" | "After these things" | "After this" | "After these things" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; the Greek meta tauta is plural ("these things"), not singular; PNEUMA's "this" loses the plural |
| 1 | "he-came to Corinth" vs. "he came to Corinth" vs. "Paul left Athens and went to Corinth" | "he-came to Corinth" | "he came to Corinth" | "Paul left Athens and went to Corinth" | "he came to Corinth" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "Paul" as subject (not in the Greek) and restructures; the Greek uses the pronoun-less verb elthen |
| 2 | "of-Pontus by-race" vs. "a native of Pontus by birth" vs. "originally from Pontus" | "of-Pontus by-race" | "a native of Pontus by birth" | "originally from Pontus" | "a native of Pontus by birth" -- follows LOGOS; Pontikon to genei rendered formally; PNEUMA drops the ethnic identifier to genei entirely |
| 2 | "recently having-come" vs. "having recently come" vs. "who had recently arrived" | "recently having-come" | "having recently come" | "who had recently arrived" | "who had recently come" -- composite; preserves participial sense while clarifying the relative clause |
| 2 | Sentence structure | Hyphenated literal compound | Single flowing sentence | Multiple short sentences with supplied subject "Paul" | Single flowing sentence -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA breaks the Greek period into multiple independent sentences and supplies "Paul went to see them" where the Greek has simply "he came to them" |
| 3 | "he-remained with them, and they-were-working" vs. "he stayed with them and worked" vs. "he moved in with them and they worked side by side" | "he-remained with them, and they-were-working" | "he stayed with them and worked" | "he moved in with them and they worked side by side" | "he stayed with them and they worked" -- composite; EMET's "remained" is more literal but "stayed" is natural; PNEUMA's "moved in" and "side by side" are interpretive expansions not in the Greek |
| 4 | "was-persuading Jews and Greeks" vs. "was persuading both Jews and Greeks" vs. "working to persuade both Jews and Greeks" | "was-persuading Jews and Greeks" | "was persuading both Jews and Greeks" | "working to persuade both Jews and Greeks" | "was persuading both Jews and Greeks" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek te kai ("both...and") is preserved; PNEUMA's "working to persuade" adds an interpretive layer of effort not in the Greek epeithein |
| **5** | **"constrained by the word" -- major textual variant** | "was-being-constrained by-the word" (suneixeto to logo) | "was occupied with the word" | "threw himself entirely into preaching" | **"was constrained by the word"** -- follows EMET; Sinaiticus reads SUNEIXETO TW LOGW; sunecho means "to constrain, press, grip"; LOGOS's "occupied" softens the urgency; PNEUMA's dynamic paraphrase loses the specific Sinaiticus reading entirely; see Disagreement Log |
| 5 | "testifying to-the Jews [that] Jesus [is] the Christ" vs. same vs. "passionately testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah" | "testifying to-the Jews [that] Jesus [is] the Christ" | "testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ" | "passionately testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah" | "testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "passionately" (not in the Greek) and renders Christos as "Messiah" and changes present tense to past tense |
| 6 | "were-opposing and blaspheming" vs. "opposed and blasphemed" vs. "turned hostile and hurled insults" | "were-opposing and blaspheming" (imperfect) | "opposed and blasphemed" (aorist rendering) | "turned hostile and hurled insults at him" | "opposed and blasphemed" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "hurled insults" weakens "blasphemed" (blasphemounton specifically means speaking against God/holy things, not merely insulting Paul) |
| 6 | "having-shaken-out [his] garments" vs. "he shook out his garments" vs. "he shook out his garments" | "having-shaken-out [his] garments" | "he shook out his garments" | "he shook out his garments" | "he shook out his garments" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; the participial form is rendered as a finite verb for English clarity |
| 6 | "Your blood [be] upon your head" vs. "Your blood be upon your own head" vs. "Your blood is on your own heads" | "Your blood [be] upon your head" (singular) | "Your blood be upon your own head" (singular + "own") | "Your blood is on your own heads" (plural + indicative) | "Your blood be upon your own head" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek is singular kephalen (head); PNEUMA's plural "heads" departs from the Greek; LOGOS's "own" (hymn) is in the text |
| 6 | "I [am] clean" vs. "I am clean" vs. "I am not responsible" | "I [am] clean" (katharos) | "I am clean" | "I am not responsible" | "I am clean" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; katharos means "clean/pure"; PNEUMA's "not responsible" is an interpretation, not a translation |
| 7 | "having-moved from-there" vs. "having departed from there" vs. "So he left the synagogue and moved next door" | "having-moved from-there" | "having departed from there" | "So he left the synagogue and moved next door" | "having departed from there" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "the synagogue" and "next door" (interpretive additions not in the Greek metabas ekeithen) |
| 7 | "a certain [man] named Titius Justus" vs. "a certain man named Titius Justus" vs. "a man named Titius Justus" | "a certain [man] named Titius Justus" | "a certain man named Titius Justus" | "a man named Titius Justus" | "a certain man named Titius Justus" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek tinos ("certain") is preserved |
| 8 | "ruler-of-the-synagogue" vs. "ruler of the synagogue" vs. "synagogue leader" | "ruler-of-the-synagogue" | "ruler of the synagogue" | "synagogue leader" | "ruler of the synagogue" -- follows LOGOS; archisynagogos is a formal title; "leader" (PNEUMA) is less precise |
| 8 | "believed in-the Lord" vs. "believed in the Lord" vs. "put his trust in the Lord" | "believed in-the Lord" | "believed in the Lord" | "put his trust in the Lord" | "believed in the Lord" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; episteusen to kurio is literally "believed in the Lord"; PNEUMA's "put his trust" is interpretive paraphrase |
| 8 | "hearing were-believing and were-being-baptized" vs. "hearing, were believing and being baptized" vs. "who heard the message believed and were baptized" | "hearing were-believing and were-being-baptized" | "hearing, were believing and being baptized" | "who heard the message believed and were baptized" | "hearing, were believing and being baptized" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "the message" (not in the Greek) and loses the continuous aspect of the imperfects |
| 9 | Word order: "through a vision in [the] night to-Paul" vs. "to Paul in the night through a vision" vs. "One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision" | "through a vision in [the] night to-Paul" | "to Paul in the night through a vision" | "One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision" | "to Paul through a vision in the night" -- composite following LOGOS's word order with EMET's "through a vision" placement; PNEUMA restructures entirely into a temporal clause |
| 9 | "Do-not be-afraid, but speak and do-not be-silent" vs. "Do not be afraid, but speak, and do not be silent" vs. "Do not be afraid. Keep speaking and do not be silent" | "Do-not be-afraid, but speak and do-not be-silent" | "Do not be afraid, but speak, and do not be silent" | "Do not be afraid. Keep speaking and do not be silent" | "Do not be afraid, but speak, and do not be silent" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA breaks into two sentences and adds "Keep" (not in the Greek lali, which is simply "speak") |
| 10 | "no-one will-set-upon you to-harm you" vs. "no one will attack you to harm you" vs. "no one is going to lay a hand on you to harm you" | "will-set-upon" (epithesthai) | "will attack" | "lay a hand on" | "will attack you to harm you" -- follows LOGOS; epithesthai means "to set upon, attack"; PNEUMA's "lay a hand on" is idiomatic but adds physicality not specified; EMET's "set upon" is archaic |
| 10 | "there-is a people to-me much in this city" vs. "there are many people belonging to me in this city" vs. "I have many people in this city" | "there-is a people to-me much" (dative of possession) | "there are many people belonging to me" | "I have many people" | "there are many people belonging to me in this city" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the possessive relationship; PNEUMA's "I have" is acceptable but loses the Greek's distinctive phrasing with the dative |
| 11 | "he-sat a year and six months" vs. "he stayed a year and six months" vs. "Paul stayed for a year and six months" | "he-sat" (ekathisen) | "he stayed" | "Paul stayed" | "he stayed" -- follows LOGOS; ekathisen literally means "he sat" but idiomatically means "he settled/stayed"; PNEUMA supplies "Paul" as subject (not in the Greek) |
| 12 | "was-being proconsul" vs. "was proconsul" vs. "became proconsul" | "was-being proconsul" (anthypateuontos) | "was proconsul" | "became proconsul" | "was proconsul" -- follows LOGOS; the genitive absolute anthypateuontos indicates temporal setting ("when Gallio was proconsul"), not inception; PNEUMA's "became" implies a new appointment |
| 12 | "rose-up-against Paul and brought him before the judgment-seat" vs. "rose up against Paul and brought him before the judgment seat" vs. "made a united attack on Paul and hauled him before the tribunal" | "rose-up-against...judgment-seat" | "rose up against...judgment seat" | "made a united attack...hauled...tribunal" | "rose up against Paul and brought him before the judgment seat" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "hauled" and "tribunal" are dynamic renderings; bema is specifically a judgment seat |
| 13 | "Contrary-to the law this-[one] is-persuading men to-worship God" vs. "This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law" | "Contrary-to the law this-[one] is-persuading men" (Greek word order) | "This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law" | "This man is persuading people to worship God in ways that violate the law!" | "This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "in ways that violate" is an interpretive expansion of para ton nomon ("contrary to the law"); EMET preserves Greek word order |
| 13 | "men" vs. "people" vs. "people" | "men" (tous anthropous) | "people" | "people" | "people" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; anthropous here means "people" generically |
| 14 | "wrongdoing or wicked villainy" vs. "wrongdoing or wicked crime" vs. "crime or serious wrongdoing" | "wrongdoing or wicked villainy" (adikema/rhadiourgema) | "wrongdoing or wicked crime" | "crime or serious wrongdoing" | "wrongdoing or wicked villainy" -- follows EMET; rhadiourgema specifically means "villainy/reckless mischief," a legal term stronger than "crime"; PNEUMA reverses the order and loses the specific Greek terms |
| 14 | "according-to reason I-would-have-borne-with you" vs. "I would with reason bear with you" vs. "I would have reason to hear you out" | "according-to reason I-would-have-borne-with" | "I would with reason bear with you" | "I would have reason to hear you out" | "I would with reason bear with you" -- follows LOGOS; aneschomin means "to bear with, endure" (implying patience with a tedious matter); PNEUMA's "hear you out" loses the condescension in Gallio's tone |
| 15 | "questions about a word and names and the law according-to you" vs. "questions about a word and names and the law among you" vs. "a dispute about words and names and your own law" | "the law according-to you" | "the law among you" | "your own law" | "the law among you" -- follows LOGOS; kath' hymas means "according to/among you"; PNEUMA's "your own law" is interpretive though captures Gallio's dismissive tone |
| 15 | "you-will-see-to-[it] yourselves" vs. "see to it yourselves" vs. "deal with it yourselves" | "you-will-see-to-[it] yourselves" (future indicative) | "see to it yourselves" (imperative) | "deal with it yourselves" | "see to it yourselves" -- follows LOGOS; opsesthe is future indicative but functions as an imperative; PNEUMA's "deal with it" is colloquial |
| 15 | "a judge of these-[things] I do-not wish to-be" vs. "I do not wish to be a judge of these things" vs. "I refuse to be a judge of such things" | Greek word order (predicate first) | English word order | "I refuse" (stronger than "do not wish") | "I do not wish to be a judge of these things" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "refuse" is stronger than the Greek boulomai (wish/desire); EMET preserves Greek emphasis by fronting "a judge" |
| 16 | "he-drove them from the judgment-seat" vs. "he drove them away from the judgment seat" vs. "he had them driven away from the tribunal" | "he-drove them" | "he drove them away" | "he had them driven away from the tribunal" | "he drove them from the judgment seat" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "had them driven" implies delegation to officers (not in the Greek); and "tribunal" for bema is inconsistent with "judgment seat" |
| **17** | **"they all" -- ambiguous subject** | "they all, having-seized Sosthenes" | "they all seized Sosthenes" | "the crowd seized Sosthenes" | **"they all, having seized Sosthenes"** -- follows EMET; the Greek pantes ("all") is deliberately ambiguous; PNEUMA supplies "the crowd" which resolves an ambiguity that should be preserved; see Disagreement Log |
| 17 | "ruler-of-the-synagogue" vs. "ruler of the synagogue" vs. "synagogue leader" | "ruler-of-the-synagogue" | "ruler of the synagogue" | "synagogue leader" | "ruler of the synagogue" -- follows LOGOS; consistent with v. 8 |
| 17 | "were-beating [him]" vs. "beat him" vs. "beat him" | "were-beating" (imperfect etupton) | "beat" | "beat" | "were beating him" -- follows EMET; the imperfect etupton indicates continuous/repeated action, not a single blow; LOGOS/PNEUMA lose this aspect |
| 17 | "none of these-[things] was-a-concern to-Gallio" vs. "none of these things were of concern to Gallio" vs. "Gallio could not have cared less" | "none of these-[things] was-a-concern to-Gallio" | "none of these things were of concern to Gallio" | "Gallio could not have cared less" | "none of these things was of concern to Gallio" -- follows EMET for singular verb (ouden...emelen is singular); PNEUMA's idiom is vivid but loses the Greek's measured narrative voice |
| **18** | **"having-shorn [his] head" -- ambiguous antecedent** | "having-shorn [his] head in Cenchreae, for he-had a vow" | "having shorn his head in Cenchreae, for he had a vow" | "At Cenchreae he had his hair cut, because he was under a vow" | **"having shorn his head in Cenchreae, for he had a vow"** -- follows EMET/LOGOS; the participial keiramenos is grammatically ambiguous (could refer to Paul or Aquila) but traditionally understood as Paul; PNEUMA restructures into an independent clause and renders "had his hair cut" (causative, implying someone else cut it); see Disagreement Log |
| 18 | "having-bid-farewell to-the brothers" vs. "took leave of the brothers" vs. "said goodbye to the brothers and sisters" | "having-bid-farewell to-the brothers" | "took leave of the brothers" | "said goodbye to the brothers and sisters" | "took leave of the brothers" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" is an inclusive rendering of adelphois; while adelphois may include women, the Greek form is masculine plural; "brothers" is preserved as in previous chapters |
| 18 | "was-sailing-away to Syria" vs. "sailed away to Syria" vs. "sailed for Syria" | "was-sailing-away" (exeplei) | "sailed away" | "sailed for" | "sailed away to Syria" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "sailed for" loses the departure emphasis of the ek- prefix |
| 19 | "he-left those-[ones] there" vs. "he left them there" vs. "Paul left Priscilla and Aquila behind" | "he-left those-[ones] there" | "he left them there" | "Paul left Priscilla and Aquila behind" | "he left them there" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies the names (not in the Greek ekeinous, "those ones/them") and adds "behind" |
| 19 | "he-himself, having-entered into the synagogue, reasoned" vs. "he himself entered into the synagogue and reasoned" vs. "He himself went into the synagogue and engaged in discussion" | "having-entered...reasoned" (participial) | "entered...and reasoned" | "went into...and engaged in discussion" | "having entered into the synagogue, reasoned with the Jews" -- composite preserving participial structure from EMET with clarity from LOGOS; PNEUMA's "engaged in discussion" weakens dialegomai which means specifically to reason/argue |
| 20 | "to-remain for a longer time" vs. "to remain for a longer time" vs. "to stay longer" | "to-remain for a longer time" | "to remain for a longer time" | "to stay longer" | "to remain for a longer time" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA compresses epi pleiona chronon ("for a longer time") |
| 21 | "having-bid-farewell and having-said" vs. "took leave of them, saying" vs. "as he said farewell he told them" | "having-bid-farewell and having-said" | "took leave of them, saying" | "as he said farewell he told them" | "took leave of them, saying" -- follows LOGOS |
| **21** | **Omission of feast clause** | Omits "I must by all means keep the coming feast in Jerusalem" (follows Sinaiticus) | Omits (follows Sinaiticus) | Omits (follows Sinaiticus) | **Omits** -- unanimous agreement following Sinaiticus shorter reading; the Textus Receptus addition is not in the Alexandrian witnesses; all three translators correctly follow the manuscript |
| 21 | "I-will-return again to you" vs. "I will return to you again" vs. "I will come back to you" | "I-will-return again to you" | "I will return to you again" | "I will come back to you" | "I will return to you again" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "come back" is less formal than the Greek palindrome anakampso |
| 21 | "God willing" vs. "God willing" vs. "God willing" | "God willing" | "God willing" | "God willing" | "God willing" -- unanimous |
| 22 | "having-gone-down...having-gone-up and having-greeted the church, he-went-down" vs. "having come down...he went up and greeted the church, and went down" vs. "After landing...he went up and greeted the church, and then went down" | Three participles in sequence | Mixed construction | "After landing" restructures | "having come down to Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, and went down to Antioch" -- follows LOGOS; the sequence of anabas (going up = to Jerusalem) and katebe (going down = to Antioch) preserves the geographic up/down theology of Jerusalem; PNEUMA's "landing" loses katelthon ("having come down") |
| 23 | "having-spent some time" vs. "having spent some time there" vs. "He spent some time there" | "having-spent some time" | "having spent some time there" | "He spent some time there" | "having spent some time" -- follows EMET/LOGOS participial construction; PNEUMA breaks into independent sentence |
| 23 | "passing-through in-order the Galatian region and Phrygia" vs. "passing through the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order" vs. "traveling systematically through the region of Galatia and Phrygia" | "in-order the Galatian region and Phrygia" | "the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order" | "systematically through the region of Galatia and Phrygia" | "the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "systematically" is an interpretation of kathexes ("in order/in sequence"); the Greek indicates sequential passage, not methodical strategy |
| **24** | **"learned man" vs. "eloquent man" -- Apollos characterization** | "a learned man" (aner logios) | "an eloquent man" | "an eloquent man with a powerful command of the Scriptures" | **"an eloquent man"** -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); the Greek logios can mean either "learned/educated" or "eloquent"; in the context of Apollos's subsequent public debating (v. 28) and powerful speech, "eloquent" captures the rhetorical dimension; EMET's "learned" is defensible but less apt; PNEUMA's expansion "with a powerful command" merges v. 24b into the characterization; see Disagreement Log |
| 24 | "Apollos [by] name, an Alexandrian by-race" vs. "named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth" vs. "named Apollos...from Alexandria" | "Apollos [by] name, an Alexandrian by-race" | "named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth" | "named Apollos...from Alexandria" | "named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA drops to genei ("by birth/race") |
| **25** | **"the way of the Lord" vs. "the Way of the Lord" -- capitalization and manuscript reading** | "the way of-the Lord" (ten hodon tou kyriou) | "the way of the Lord" | "the Way of the Lord" (capitalized) | **"the way of the Lord"** -- follows EMET/LOGOS; Sinaiticus reads THN ODON TOU KURIOU; scriptio continua has no capitalization distinction; PNEUMA's capitalization of "Way" implies a technical term (cf. Acts 9:2) which may be valid but exceeds what the manuscript indicates here |
| 25 | "having-been-instructed" vs. "had been instructed" vs. "had been instructed" | "having-been-instructed" (en katechumenos) | "had been instructed" | "had been instructed" | "had been instructed" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; the periphrastic pluperfect is rendered naturally |
| 25 | "being-fervent in-the spirit" vs. "being fervent in spirit" vs. "with burning enthusiasm" | "being-fervent in-the spirit" (zeon to pneumati) | "being fervent in spirit" | "with burning enthusiasm" | "being fervent in spirit" -- follows LOGOS; zeon to pneumati literally means "boiling in spirit"; PNEUMA's "burning enthusiasm" captures the thermal metaphor but loses the word "spirit" (pneuma) |
| 25 | "knowing only the baptism of-John" vs. "though knowing only the baptism of John" vs. "though he knew only the baptism of John" | "knowing only" (no concessive marker) | "though knowing only" (adds concessive) | "though he knew only" (adds concessive + finite verb) | "though knowing only the baptism of John" -- follows LOGOS; the concessive "though" is implied by the context (accurate teaching despite limited knowledge) even if not syntactically marked in the Greek; EMET's bare participial is less clear in English |
| **26** | **"Priscilla and Aquila" -- name order** | "Priscilla and Aquila" | "Priscilla and Aquila" | "Priscilla and Aquila" | **"Priscilla and Aquila"** -- unanimous agreement following Sinaiticus, which places Priscilla first; this reversed order (wife before husband) is a notable feature of the manuscript and may indicate Priscilla's prominence in the teaching ministry |
| 26 | "took-him-aside and more-accurately expounded" vs. "took him aside and explained...more accurately" vs. "took him aside and explained...more accurately" | "took-him-aside and more-accurately expounded" (proselabonto/exethento) | "took him aside and explained...more accurately" | "took him aside and explained...more accurately" | "took him aside and more accurately expounded" -- follows EMET; exethento (from ektithemi) means "to set forth, expound" -- a word that implies detailed, systematic explanation, stronger than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "explained" |
| **26** | **"the way of God" -- manuscript reading** | "the way of-God" (ten hodon tou theou) | "the way of God" | "the Way of God" (capitalized) | **"the way of God"** -- unanimous on reading; Sinaiticus reads TOU QEOU ("of God") here in v. 26, distinct from TOU KURIOU ("of the Lord") in v. 25; this variation (Lord in v. 25 vs. God in v. 26) is preserved; PNEUMA again capitalizes "Way" |
| 27 | "the brothers having-encouraged [him] wrote" vs. "the brothers encouraged him and wrote" vs. "the brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote" | "the brothers having-encouraged [him] wrote" | "the brothers encouraged him and wrote" | "the brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote" | "the brothers encouraged him and wrote" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "and sisters" (adelphoi is masculine plural); EMET's participial subordination is more literal |
| 27 | "to-receive him" vs. "to welcome him" vs. "to welcome him" | "to-receive" (apodexasthai) | "to welcome" | "to welcome" | "to welcome him" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); apodechomai means "to welcome, receive favorably" -- "welcome" captures the warmth |
| 27 | "contributed much" vs. "greatly helped" vs. "proved to be a tremendous asset" | "contributed much" (synebaleto poly) | "greatly helped" | "proved to be a tremendous asset" | "greatly helped" -- follows LOGOS; synebaleto poly means "contributed greatly/helped much"; EMET's "contributed" is more literal but less clear in context; PNEUMA's "tremendous asset" is a dynamic expansion |
| 27 | "to-the-[ones] having-believed through grace" vs. "those who had believed through grace" vs. "those who had come to faith through grace" | "to-the-[ones] having-believed through grace" | "those who had believed through grace" | "those who had come to faith through grace" | "those who had believed through grace" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the ambiguity of dia tes charitos (could modify "believed" or "helped"); PNEUMA's "come to faith" is interpretive expansion of pisteukotes |
| 28 | "powerfully he-was-thoroughly-refuting" vs. "he was powerfully refuting" vs. "he vigorously refuted" | "powerfully he-was-thoroughly-refuting" (eutonos diakatelegcheto) | "he was powerfully refuting" | "he vigorously refuted" | "he was powerfully refuting" -- follows LOGOS; eutonos means "powerfully/vigorously" (both are acceptable); the imperfect diakatelegcheto indicates ongoing action; PNEUMA's aorist "refuted" loses this continuous aspect |
| 28 | "publicly" vs. "publicly" vs. "in public debate" | "publicly" (demosiai) | "publicly" | "in public debate" | "publicly" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "in public debate" interprets the adverb as a setting |
| 28 | "showing through the Scriptures [that] Jesus is the Christ" vs. same vs. "demonstrating from the Scriptures that the Messiah was Jesus" | "showing...Jesus is the Christ" | "showing...that Jesus is the Christ" | "demonstrating...that the Messiah was Jesus" | "showing through the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA reverses the predicate ("the Messiah was Jesus" vs. "Jesus is the Christ") and renders Christos as "Messiah"; while "Messiah" is the semantic equivalent, the SOPHIA Bible preserves "Christ" as the consistent rendering of Christos in Acts |

-- SOPHIA
